Author: Julia Diez
Title: Claude Code: A super simple guide
Text:
Claude Code: A super simple guide
No coding degree required
Imagine you had a super smart robot friend who could live inside your computer. You talk to it in plain English. It reads your files, writes code, fixes bugs, and even saves its work, all while you watch. That’s basically what Claude Code is.
Whether you’re a developer, a curious beginner, or someone who just heard people buzzing about it, this guide will explain everything in plain language, with real examples you can actually use.
What Exactly Is Claude Code?
Claude Code is an AI assistant made by Anthropic. Here’s what makes it special: instead of living in a chat window like most AI tools, it lives right in your terminal (that black screen with blinking text you’ve seen developers use in movies).
Think of it like this:
Regular AI chatbot = a smart friend you can text
Claude Code = a smart friend who can also walk into your house, read all your notebooks, fix your homework, and organize your room
It can read your files, write and edit code, run commands, and work with GitHub (a place where code lives online) — all from a single conversation.
How Does It Actually Work?
Here’s the simple version:
You type a question or request in plain English
Claude Code reads your project files to understand what’s going on
It makes a plan, shows you what it’s thinking, and asks permission before changing anything
You say yes (or no), and it does the work
It tells you what it did
That’s it. No magic spells. No complicated menus. Just talking.
Getting Started: Install It in 3 Steps
Before you can use Claude Code, you need two things on your computer:
Node.js (version 18 or newer) — think of this as a “power adapter” Claude Code needs to run
A Claude subscription or Anthropic account
Once you have those, open your terminal and type:
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
Then start it with:
claude
That’s literally it. You’re in. 🎉
5 Real Things You Can Do With Claude Code (With Examples!)
1. Understand a Project You’ve Never Seen Before
Got handed a pile of code you didn’t write and have no idea what it does? Just ask.
You type:
“Explain what this project does and how it’s organized.”
What Claude does: It reads through all your files, figures out the structure, and gives you a plain-English summary. It even follows the connections between files — like reading a book and following every footnote automatically.
2. Fix a Bug Using Plain English
You don’t need to know exactly where the problem is. Just describe what’s going wrong.
You type:
“Users are getting a weird error when they try to log in after clicking ‘forgot password.’ Can you find and fix the bug?”
What Claude does: It searches through your code, finds the likely cause, explains it to you, and asks if it can fix it. You say yes, it fixes it.
3. Write New Features From Scratch
Want to add something new to your app? Describe it.
You type:
“Add a dark mode button to the top right corner of the homepage that saves the user’s preference.”
What Claude does: It figures out where to put the button, writes the code, adds the “save preference” logic, and shows you the changes before committing them.
4. Handle Git (the Save Button for Code)
Git is how developers save and share code. It can be confusing. Claude Code makes it easy.
You type:
“Commit all my recent changes with a good message and push it to GitHub.”
What Claude does: It stages the changes, writes a clear commit message describing what changed, and pushes it — all without you having to remember a single Git command.
5. Clean Up Messy Code
Got code that works but looks like a tornado hit it?
You type:
“Refactor the functions in this file to be cleaner and add comments explaining what each one does.”
What Claude does: It rewrites the code to be cleaner, adds helpful comments, and makes sure it still does the same thing it did before.
The Secret Weapon: CLAUDE.md
Here’s a little trick that makes Claude Code way more powerful. You can create a file called CLAUDE.md in your project folder. Claude reads this file every single time it starts a new session.
Think of it like leaving a sticky note for your robot friend:
# My Project Notes for Claude
- This is a React app using TypeScript
- Always run `npm test` before committing
- Never touch the /legacy folder
- Prefer arrow functions over regular functions
Now Claude knows the rules of your project without you having to explain it every single time. It’s like leaving instructions for a house sitter — super useful.
The “Explore, Plan, Code, Commit” Method
The smartest way to use Claude Code for big tasks is a four-step dance:
Step 1 — Explore: Tell Claude to just read the files first. Don’t make any changes yet.
“Read through the authentication files and understand how login currently works. Don’t change anything.”
Step 2 — Plan: Ask Claude to write out a plan before it touches any code.
“Now write a detailed plan for how you’d add two-factor authentication.”
Step 3 — Code: Once the plan looks good, tell it to go ahead.
“Great, now implement that plan.”
Step 4 — Commit: Save the work.
“Commit these changes with a descriptive message.”
This method stops Claude from diving in headfirst and building the wrong thing. It’s like looking at a recipe before cooking instead of just guessing.
Useful Commands to Know
These are like shortcuts. Type them right in the chat:
Command What it does /help
Shows a list of all commands /review-pr
Reviews a pull request (code someone wants to merge) Escape
Stops Claude mid-task Escape + Escape
Shows a list of your previous messages
You can even create your own custom commands for things your team does over and over. Like /deploy-to-staging
or /run-all-tests
.
Where Can You Use It?
Claude Code isn’t just for the terminal anymore. You can use it in:
VS Code (the most popular code editor)
JetBrains IDEs (other popular code editors)
A web browser — just go to claude.ai
Your iPhone — yes, there’s an app
Slack — mention @Claude in a channel and get a pull request back
And the cool part: your CLAUDE.md settings and history follow you across all of them. Start on your phone during lunch, finish in your terminal at your desk.
A Few Things to Know
Claude Code is amazing, but here are a few real things to keep in mind:
It asks permission... a lot. Before editing a file or running a command, it’ll ask. This is a good thing — it means no surprises. Some people get used to it quickly; others find it a little slow at first.
It can make mistakes. Always review what Claude changes before accepting it, especially in important projects. Think of it like a very talented intern: brilliant, fast, but still needs a human to double-check the work.
It uses API credits. The more complex the task, the more it costs. Keep an eye on your usage if you’re on a paid plan.
Who Is This Actually For?
Claude Code started as a tool for software developers, but it’s spread way beyond that. Data scientists use it to write queries and build charts. Writers use it to organize notes. Non-technical people use it to build simple websites and apps without knowing any code, “vibe coding.”
If you can describe what you want in plain English, there’s a good chance Claude Code can help you build it.
Claude Code and Localization — Beyond Translation
Here’s where Claude Code does something most localization tools can’t even attempt.
When most people think about translating an app, they think word-for-word: take the English button, make it Spanish, done. But that’s not how real markets work. A bedroom producer in Madrid has a completely different relationship with music software than one in Chicago — different vocabulary expectations, different anxiety about AI and authorship, different tolerance for hype, different character limits on their phone screen.
The workflow described below doesn’t translate strings. It rewrites them for the target audience, grounded in a full semantic understanding of the product, the market, and the UI context. Claude Code is the engine that runs it.
The Semantic Layer: Teaching Claude Who It’s Talking To
The foundation of this workflow is a structured knowledge base that lives in your project and loads automatically every time Claude Code starts a session — through CLAUDE.md.
CLAUDE.md points Claude to the whole ontology: an index of concept files (one per product term), market files (one per locale), a semantic relationship graph, and a class definitions table that controls translation policy. Claude reads all of this before it writes a single word.
The market files are where the real work lives. For each locale they define:
Voice DNA — the personality Claude must write in. For Spain: cercana (approachable), experta (credible), motivadora (encouraging). For the US: confident, tech-forward, creator-first. These aren’t adjectives on a mood board, they’re operating constraints.
Tone rules — what to do and what never to do. For Spain, words like “revolucionario,” “mágico,” or “sin esfuerzo” are explicitly banned. They sound like replacement-level AI hype, and Spanish creators are sensitive to that. For the US: no corporate-speak, no exclamation marks in system messages.
Cultural pragmatics — the beliefs and anxieties that shape how your audience reads. Spanish creators have real concern about AI replacing their authorship. So every AI feature in that market needs an agency cue baked in: “tú decides,” “puedes ajustar,” “elige.” Not because legal said so — because that’s what makes the product trustworthy to that user.
Terminology rules — the exact words the brand uses in that market, locked in. “Track” is always “pista.” “Mix” is always “mezcla.” The cloud is always “la nube.” No drifting between synonyms. No LatAm vocabulary creeping in.
Hard UI constraints — character budgets tied to real screen space. Buttons ≤ 22 characters when possible. Error titles ≤ 45 characters. Tooltips ≤ 120. These aren’t style preferences — they’re pixel budgets.
When Claude reads all of this at session start, it isn’t loading preferences. It’s inhabiting a market persona with guardrails tight enough to produce consistent, on-brand output at scale.
Context That Travels With the Term
The class definitions table tells Claude how to treat each type of concept. Features have a translation policy of translate
. Plans and Plan Groups have a policy of do_not_translate
. So when Claude encounters a Plan name like “Creator Starter” inside a sentence, it knows to leave that string untouched, even mid-copy. The class handles it automatically.
But the ID tells Claude more than policy. It tells Claude what the concept is and what it connects to. The semantic relationship graph shows that the MIDI Editor (feat_00000001
) is_feature_of
Creator Starter (plan_00000002
). Creator Starter is_part_of_plan_group
Creator — the plan group designed for solo musicians and producers building music entirely in the cloud. That’s not a translation note. That’s product context that changes what the output should say and how the voice should land.
When Claude writes the es-es description for the MIDI Editor, it knows it’s talking to a Spain-market bedroom producer looking at an entry-level feature on a subscription landing page. That’s a completely different job than writing a tooltip for a power user inside a DAW. Same concept ID, different surface — the string changes accordingly.
Original Length as a UI Constraint
Source string length isn’t just metadata — it’s a signal about where in the UI this text lives and how much space the target string has to work with.
An 11-character English label like “MIDI Editor” signals a compact UI element. A 52-character feature description signals more room. Claude uses this alongside the hard character limits in the market file to make output decisions. If a natural es-es rewrite of a tooltip runs to 135 characters and the market file caps tooltips at 120, Claude doesn’t just trim randomly — it rethinks the sentence structure to stay within budget while keeping the meaning and tone intact.
This is what makes the output deployable, not just linguistically correct.
What Gets Produced Is Not a Translation
A translation asks: what does this say in another language?
This workflow asks: what would a Spain-market creator expect to read here, in this UI element, for this feature, in this brand voice, at this character length?
Here’s a concrete example using Echocraft, a fictional cloud music production platform. The English feature description for the Pattern Sequencer might read:
“Build beats and patterns visually, then arrange them into a full song.”
A word-for-word translation is technically correct but flat. This is a typical word-for-word translation:
"Crea ritmos y patrones de forma visual, luego organízalos en una canción completa."
Instead, what this new workflow produces is grounded in the es-es voice DNA — short imperative verbs, no hype, outcome-driven, with the authorship signal that Spain-market creators need:
“Crea patrones y ritmos de forma visual. Tú decides cómo se organiza la canción.”
It’s shorter. It uses imperatives (”Crea”). It adds the agency cue (”Tú decides”). It fits the character budget. Nothing about it is a translation — it’s a market-fit rewrite produced from semantic context, market persona, and UI constraints working together.
For a feature with an AI angle, the difference is even sharper. The es-es market file is explicit: whenever AI is mentioned, include a control cue. So “AI suggests melodies based on your style” doesn’t become a literal Spanish equivalent. It becomes:
“La IA propone melodías según tu estilo. Tú eliges qué se queda.”
The added clause isn’t padding, it’s a cultural requirement for that market.
How the Workflow Runs in Claude Code
Once the semantic layer is in place, the workflow runs through four prompts:
Step 1 — Audit gaps:
“Read the ontology, the es-es market file, and all terminology files. List every feature with a source term in en-us but no approved term in es-es. Include the concept ID and class for each.”
Claude navigates the file structure, checks translation policy per class, and returns a precise gap list with IDs — not just names.
Step 2 — Generate market-fit target strings:
“For each feature on that list, write a target string for the es-es market. Use the voice DNA, tone rules, terminology, and UI constraints from the es-es market file. Don’t translate — rewrite for the audience. Return each result with its concept ID, the original string length, and your output character count.”
Step 3 — Surface decisions that need a human:
“Flag any string where you had to choose between terminology options, where an AI feature required an agency cue, or where hitting the character limit changed the meaning. Explain your reasoning for each.”
Claude doesn’t silently make judgment calls — it surfaces them with reasoning written out, so a reviewer approves or redirects rather than checking everything from scratch.
Step 4 — Write approved terms back to the knowledge base:
“Update the terminology files for each approved concept with the es-es term and status ‘pending_review’. Note the output character count in the file.”
The ontology stays current. The next time a new feature is added and the workflow runs, already-approved terms are in place and the semantic relationships still hold.
Why This Changes What Localization Means
The traditional model treats localization as a downstream task: build in English, translate later, fix inconsistencies when QA catches them. It’s slow, it drifts, and it produces text that sounds like it was translated rather than written for the person reading it.
What this workflow makes possible is localization that’s part of the development loop — where every new string is treated as a product decision for each market. Claude Code makes it work because it holds the full semantic context in memory at once: product structure, brand voice per locale, market persona, UI constraints, and the terminology graph — all loaded from files that live in the project and travel with the codebase.
The result isn’t a translated app. It’s an app that was written for each market, using the same source of truth, with the same intentionality as the original.
Claude Code is an AI coding assistant that lives in your terminal (and browser, and phone). It reads your files, writes and fixes code, handles Git, and takes instructions in plain English. You stay in control — it always asks before making changes.
The best way to get started is to install it, open a project you’re working on, and just start asking it questions. You’ll be surprised how much it can do.
For the full official documentation, visit: code.claude.com
Black Ice is a semantic infrastructure platform that structures your product knowledge — terminology, market rules, and concept relationships — so AI systems always generate accurate, consistent content.
Think of it as the invisible foundation beneath your AI workflows. Without it, terminology drifts, translations diverge, and agents contradict each other. With it, every LLM, localization pipeline, and content system works from the same governed source of truth.
Black Ice exports your entire ontology as a structured markdown folder (ZIP), purpose-built for AI coding agents like Claude Code.
The export generates a CLAUDE.md index file alongside organized markdown files covering your concepts, terminology, market definitions, relationships, and classes. Drop it into your project root, and your AI coding agent instantly understands your product’s terminology, what’s approved, what’s available in each market, and how concepts relate to each other — no prompt engineering required.
Available filters let you export the full ontology or a single market bundle, and restrict to approved terms only — so your agent always works with production-ready language.
For requesting early access, visit black-ice.ai
